sections.paragraph:
-

(1) pr. trans. to make long, to extend, to stretch out, kindred to the root עָרַךְ which see. Hence אֲרוּכָה a long bandage; Syr. ܐܶܪܰܟ to prolong, Arab. أَرَكَ to tarry, to delay in a matter.

(2) Med. E. fut. יֶאֱרַךְ; pl. יַאַרְכוּ intransit. to be long. (Syr., Arab., Sam. id.; Aph. אֹורִיךְ, ܐܰܘܪܶܟ to prolong.) Eze 31:5 Gen 26:8, וַיְהִי בִּי אָֽרְכוּ־לֹו שָׁם הַיָּמִים “and it came to pass when days were prolonged to him there,” i.e. when he had lived there long; Eze 12:22.

Hiphil הֶאֱרִיךְ-

(1) to make long, lengthen, prolong, Psa 129:3 “to put forth the tongue,” Isa 57:4 הֶאֱרִיךְ יְמֵי פ׳ “to prolong any one’s life,” to grant him long life, 1Ki 3:14 on the other hand ה׳ יָמָיו “to prolong one’s own life,” to be long-lived, Deu 4:26, 40 5:30 17:20 22:7 Isa 53:10 also without יָמִים Pro 28:2 Ecc 7:15 8:12 .

(2) intrans. to be long, 1Ki 8:8, especially used of time; הֶאֱרִיכוּ יָמָיו “to be long-lived,” Exo 20:12 Deu 5:16 6:2 25:15 compare No.1.

(3) to retard, to delay, to defer; הֶאֱרִיךְ אַפֹּו Isa 48:9 Pro 19:11, to defer anger, to be patient, and μακρόθυμος. [“So too הֶאֱרִיךְ נַפְשֹׁו id., Job 6:11.” Ges. add.]. Compare אֶרֶךְ אַפַּיִם under אָרֵךְ.

(4) to delay, to tarry (prop. es lange machen), Num 9:19, 22 .

The derivatives, except אֲרוּכָה, immediately follow.
